segments.s191-g1: This is a nominal phrase + verb + temporal phrase: 'تخوار سراینده' = 'the wretched poet' (noun + apposition), followed by the past verb 'گفت' (he said). 'آن زمان' is a fixed temporal expression 'that time' (= 'then') placed after the verb for emphasis; word order is subject → verb → time, common in Persian narrative.
segments.s191-g2: This is a subordinate clause introduced by the relative/conjunction 'که' here meaning 'when/that'. 'آمد' is the simple past 'came'. 'بر' is the preposition 'upon/to'. 'کوه کوهی دمان' names the place: 'کوه' (mountain) with 'کوهی' giving an indefinite/qualifying sense ('a certain mountain' / 'mountainous'), followed by the proper name 'دمان' (the mountain’s name or its foothill). The phrase functions as the content of what was said.
segments.s191-g3: 'سپهدار' is a title 'commander'. 'طوسست' is a compact expression: the name 'طوس' plus the copular/possessive-like suffix '-ست' meaning 'it is Tūs' / 'is Tūs' (so: 'the commander is Tūs' or 'Tūs, the commander'). 'کامد' is an archaic/past form meaning 'came/arrived' and 'بجنگ' is an uninflected infinitival/adverbial phrase 'to/into battle' (بِ + جنگ), giving the motion/purpose.
segments.s191-g4: This clause addresses someone: 'نتابی' is from تابیدن/tāb — here used in the sense 'to endure/stand' (in classical usage often with a negative implication 'cannot stand'). 'تو' is the personal pronoun 'you'. 'با' is the preposition 'with/by'. 'کار' here means 'deed' or 'exploit'. 'دیده' is a past-participial/adjectival form 'seen' or 'witnessed' modifying 'کار' (i.e. 'deeds that have been seen/noted'), and 'نهنگ' is metaphorical for a mighty man/giant; the whole means 'you cannot match/endure the deeds seen of the giant.' Word order is verb → subject → prepositional phrase describing what cannot be withstood.
segments.s191-g5: 'برو' is the imperative 'go'. 'تا' introduces purpose here ('so that / in order that'). 'در' is used as 'door/gate' (not the locative 'in') and combines with 'دژ' (fortress) to mean 'the gate of the fortress'. 'ببندیم' is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present (let us close / may we close) used after 'تا' to express purpose. 'سخت' is an adverbial modifier 'firmly/tightly'.
segments.s191-g6: 'ببینیم' is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present 'let us see' or 'we shall see'. Again 'تا' marks purpose/result 'so that'. 'چی' is the interrogative 'what'. 'ست' is the copula 'is' (classical literary form). 'فرجام' is 'end/outcome' and 'بخت' is 'fortune/fate' — together 'چیست فرجام بخت' = 'what is the outcome of fortune' (i.e. what will be the end decided by fate).
segments.s191-g7: 'چو' here means 'when/since'. 'فرزند و داماد' are the coordinated nouns 'son and son‑in‑law'. 'او را' marks possession/definiteness ('his' + object marker 'ra'), making them 'his son and son‑in‑law' as the affected persons. 'برزم' is an archaic adverb/locution referring to 'in the battle/field' (classical usage), indicating the context in which they were ruined; the whole phrase sets the temporal/situational clause 'when/inasmuch as you (have) in battle ...'.
segments.s191-g8: 'تبه کردی' is the past 'you did harm/ruined' (تبه = ruin/evil, کردی = you did). 'اکنون' is the temporal adverb 'now'. 'میندیش' is an imperative/hortative form 'think' (classical form often used to advise). 'بزم' is the noun 'feast/assembly' and functions as the object of 'think' (i.e. 'now think about the feast' — a shift to considering celebration or consequence).
segments.s191-g17: تو هم یک سواری اگر ز آهنی is a conditional nominal phrase where the copula is omitted. تو هم = 'you too / you yourself' (emphatic subject); یک سواری = 'one rider/horseman' (noun phrase); اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. ز + آهنی is a poetic adjectival phrase 'of/made of iron' modifying سواری. The main verb that completes the condition appears in the next segment (برکنی).
segments.s191-g18: همی ... برکنی contains the finite verb: همی is a poetic preverbal particle marking ongoing/attested action (often giving a present/future feel) and برکنی is an archaic present/subjunctive 2nd-person form 'you tear / you would tear up'. کوه خارا = 'the flinty mountain' (کوه + adjective خارا). ز بن = 'from the root/base' (ز = 'from', بن = 'base, root'). Together the clause completes the conditional begun earlier: 'if ... you would tear the flinty mountain up from its root.'
segments.s191-g19: از ایرانیان نامور سی هزار is a quantified noun phrase: از ایرانیان = 'from/amongs the Iranians', نامور modifies them 'renowned', and سی هزار gives the number 'thirty thousand' (postposed numerals are normal in poetry). This whole phrase is the subject of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s191-g20: برزم تو آیند places the action: برزم is an archaic/poetic locative/adverbial relating to the battlefield or 'for battle', تو is the (emphatic/target) 'you' (marked as the one they come against), and آیند is the 3rd-person plural present/archaic verb 'they come' or 'will come'. Word order is inverted for emphasis: the adverbial (برزم) and object (تو) precede the verb آیند.
segments.s191-g21: نه دژ ماند اینجا نه سنگ و نه خاک is a series of coordinated negatives: نه ... نه ... نه = 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'. دژ ماند اینجا = 'no fortress remains here' (ماند is the verb 'remain'). سنگ و خاک continue the negation as further objects ('nor stone, nor earth/soil').
segments.s191-g22: سراسر ز جا اندر آرند پاک is a resultative/clarifying clause: سراسر = 'throughout' or 'entirely', ز جا اندر آرند is an archaic construction meaning 'they remove from their places' (ز = 'from', جا = 'place', اندر آرند = 'take/bring within' – here used idiomatically), and پاک here functions adverbially 'completely/cleanly'. The verb آرند is archaic 3rd-person plural.
segments.s191-g23: وگر طوس را زین گزندی رسد is a conditional clause: وگر = 'but if / and if', طوس را uses the object marker را to mark Tūs as the experiencer/affected party, زین گزندی = 'from this harm' (poetic demonstrative + گزندی 'harm'), and رسد is an archaic impersonal/third-person form 'befalls / happens to'. The clause means 'if this harm befalls Tūs'.
segments.s191-g24: به خسرو ز دردش نژندی رسد is the consequence clause: به خسرو = 'to Khosrow' (dative/experiencer), ز دردش = 'because of/from his pain/sorrow' (ز = 'from', درد + ش = 'his pain'), نژندی = 'disgrace/shame', and رسد = 'will come / befall' (archaic third-person). Word order places the affected person and cause before the result noun and verb.
segments.s191-g9: فرود جوان is a name + epithet ('Farud, the young man'). تیز شد is a past-tense/perfect verbal phrase meaning 'became keen/eager' (تیز = 'keen/eager' here, شد = 'became'). با + noun (با تخوار) marks accompaniment: 'along with Takhvar' (تخوار is used as a personal epithet/name here).
segments.s191-g10: که introduces the following subordinate clause ('for/that/when'); چون here has a comparative/temporal sense ('as/when'); رزم پیش آید literally 'battle comes forward' (پیش آید = 'comes/arrives'); و links the parallel noun کارزار ('combat, battlefield'). The clause gives the reason/condition for becoming eager.
segments.s191-g11: The repeated چه ... و چه ... construction is an emphatic enumerative ('be it X or Y or Z'). طوس is the proper name Tūs; شیر and پیل enumerate powerful fighters/animals. ژیان qualifies پیل (literally 'life' but here part of an epithet 'elephant-like/mighty').
segments.s191-g12: The initial چه is exclamatory ('what/what a ...'). جنگی here functions as a warlike/fighter quality ('a warrior/fighter'), followed by two striking metaphors — نهنگ (whale/huge one) and ببر (tiger) — and بیان used as a noun meaning 'manifestation/expression' (i.e. 'so displayed' or 'thus revealed'). The phrase emphasizes tremendous martial might.
segments.s191-g13: بجنگ is an adverbial/infinitival locution meaning 'in battle' or 'when fighting'. اندرون مرد را uses the object marker را after مرد ('a man's inside/innermost part'), and دل دهند is a present/plural verbal phrase 'they give (a) heart' — idiomatically 'they put courage into a man's heart'.
segments.s191-g14: The clause is negative (نه ... نهند = 'neither ... nor do they place'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetry: تیز ('sharp' / 'the sharp one') precedes its locative complements. بر آتش and بر گل are two locatives ('on fire' and 'on clay'), and نهند (from نهاد) is the plural present/imperative-form 'they place/put'. The sense: they do not set/temper the sharp (thing) on fire nor put it on clay.
segments.s191-g15: چنین گفت is a simple phrase 'thus he spoke/said'. با شاهزاده marks the addressee ('to/with the prince'); تخوار is the prince's epithet/name. Note: گفت is the verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses list gives noun senses, so here it functions as the past verb).
segments.s191-g16: که introduces reported speech ('that'); شاهان سخن را ندارند خوار is a nominal clause meaning 'kings do not have speech as low/despised' i.e. 'kings do not despise words' (ندارند here is the present plural 'do not have/hold', خوار is predicative 'despised/worthless'). The object marker را attaches to سخن.
segments.s191-g25: بکین is a poetic imperative (variant of بکن) meaning “do/make (so)”; here it issues a command to bring about the following event. پدرت is پدر + the 2nd-person possessive suffix -ت (“your father”). اندر آید is a verb phrase (‘come/happen upon’) with اندر = “upon/in” preceding the verb (an archaic/pre-classical word order), and شکست is the noun ‘defeat’ that is the event being made to ‘come’ on him.
segments.s191-g26: شکستی is the indefinite/qualifying form of شکست (“a defeat”); که introduces a relative clause “which.” هرگز is the negative adverb “never.” نشایدش is the archaic impersonal/modal نشاید (‘it is not fitting/possible’) with the enclitic pronoun -ش referring back to شکستی. بست (from بستن “to bind/close”) is used metaphorically here — literally “it cannot be bound/closed,” i.e. it cannot be repaired or undone.
segments.s191-g27: بگردان is an imperative ‘turn!’ (from گردانیدن/گرداندن) commanding control of the beast; عنان is its direct object ‘the reins.’ و simply connects the two imperatives. مینداز is an imperative ‘throw/cast!’ and تیر is its object ‘the arrow.’ The verbs are imperatives addressed to the rider; object follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s191-g28: بدژ is an adjective ‘wicked/evil’ placed before شو, the imperative ‘become!’ (شو = be). مبر is a conventional negative imperative meaning ‘do not take/do not bring’ (a fossilized negation form). رنج is the object ‘suffering/pain,’ and بر is the preposition ‘upon/against,’ marking the target خیره‌خیر. خیره‌خیر is a descriptive noun/adjective (here ‘the fortunate’); the clause says: become fierce, yet do not inflict suffering upon the fortunate.
segments.s191-g29: سخن ‘speech/words’ is the theme; هرچ (‘whatever’) qualifies it. از پیش ‘beforehand’ sets the temporal frame. بایست is an archaic/modal verb meaning ‘ought/must,’ and گفت here functions with بایست to mean ‘ought to be said’ (báyast + گفت conveys a necessity that something be said). The phrase therefore expresses that certain words should have been spoken earlier.
segments.s191-g30: نگفت is the negative past of گفتن ‘he did not say (it).’ و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ همی is an archaic adverb meaning ‘still’/‘indeed’ and here emphasizes continuing action. داشت + اندر نهفت is a periphrastic construction: داشت (was/had) + اندر نهفت (kept/hidden inside) = ‘he kept it hidden’ (داشت frames the progressive/continuous sense). نهفت is the past/participial form ‘hidden/concealed.’
segments.s191-g31: ز is the preposition ‘from’/‘because of’ (poetic). بی‌مایه is an adjective ‘without substance/worthless’ modifying دستور ‘order/command.’ ناکاردان is an attributive noun/adjective ‘incompetent’ (either describing the person who gave the orders or the quality of the orders); the whole phrase yields ‘from the incompetent’s worthless orders’ or ‘by reason of worthless, incompetent orders.’
segments.s191-g32: ورا is a prepositional/enclitic form meaning ‘to/for him’ or ‘on him.’ جنگ is the subject ‘war/battle.’ سود آمد is a verb phrase ‘benefit/profit came’ (جنگ is followed by the verbal predicate آمد). The conjunction و links the second coordinated predicate جان زیان: the verb آمد is understood to apply to both coordinated parts, so the clause reads literally ‘war brought profit, and (brought) loss of life’ (جان = life; زیان = loss/harm).

sentences.s191-s1: The wretched poet said at that time: when he came upon the foothill of a mountain called Daman.
sentences.s191-s2: The commander Tūs came to the fight; you cannot endure (or match) the deeds witnessed of the giant.
sentences.s191-s3: Go, so that we may shut the fortress gate tightly; let us see what the end of fortune is.
sentences.s191-s4: When you ruined his son and son‑in‑law in the fighting, now consider the feast.
sentences.s191-s9: If you yourself are but a single iron-clad horseman, you would still tear the flinty mountain up from its very root.
sentences.s191-s10: Thirty thousand renowned Iranians will come against you in battle on the mountainside.
sentences.s191-s11: No fortress will remain here, nor stone nor soil; throughout they will cleanly remove them from their places.
sentences.s191-s12: And if any harm befalls Tūs because of this, to Khosrow, from his pain, disgrace will befall.
sentences.s191-s5: Farud the young man grew eager along with Takhvar, for when battle and the field of combat come forward.
sentences.s191-s6: Be it Tūs or a lion or a mighty elephant — what warriors! a whale and what a tiger revealed.
sentences.s191-s7: In battle they put heart into a man's breast; they do not set the sharp (blade) upon fire, nor place it on clay.
sentences.s191-s8: Thus he said to Prince Takhvar: that kings do not hold speech in contempt.
sentences.s191-s13: Cause defeat to come upon your father — a defeat that can never be mended.
sentences.s191-s14: Turn the reins and cast the arrow; become fierce, but do not lay suffering upon the fortunate.
sentences.s191-s15: Whatever needed to be said beforehand ought to have been said — he did not say it, and he kept it hidden.
sentences.s191-s16: Because of the incompetent’s worthless orders, the war brought advantage to him and loss of life.
